Stage Sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched 1 the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus the address part of the instruction, if any, is placed in the MAR (memory address register) the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched [6] 12 0478/11/M/J/15
mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) 2/3 matches â€“ 2 marks 1 match â€“ 1 mark [2] address bus control bus data bus this bus carries signals used to coordinate the computerâ€™s activities this bi-directional bus is used to exchange data between processor, memory and input/output devices this uni-directional bus carries signals relating to memory addresses between processor and memory Page 8 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0478 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (b) description of stage sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) 3 the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed 7 the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched (1) the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) 4 the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus 2 the address part of the instruction is placed in the MAR (memory address register) 6 the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched 5* The incrementation of the program counter can appear at any stage after 2. All other stages must be in the correct given order. [6]

Bus Description address bus this bus carries signals used to coordinate the computerâ€™s activities control bus this bi-directional bus is used to exchange data between processor, memory and input/ output devices data bus this uni-directional bus carries signals relating to memory addresses between processor and memory [2] 11 0478/13/M/J/15 [Turn over (b) The seven stages in a von Neumann fetch-execute cycle are shown in the table below. Stage Sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched 1 the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus the address part of the instruction, if any, is placed in the MAR (memory address register) the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched [6] 12 0478/13/M/J/15
mark_scheme_text: 7 (a) 2/3 matches â€“ 2 marks 1 match â€“ 1 mark [2] address bus control bus data bus this bus carries signals used to coordinate the computerâ€™s activities this bi-directional bus is used to exchange data between processor, memory and input/output devices this uni-directional bus carries signals relating to memory addresses between processor and memory Page 8 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0478 13 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 (b) description of stage sequence number the instruction is then copied from the memory location contained in the MAR (memory address register) and is placed in the MDR (memory data register) 3 the instruction is finally decoded and is then executed 7 the PC (program counter) contains the address of the next instruction to be fetched (1) the entire instruction is then copied from the MDR (memory data register) and placed in the CIR (current instruction register) 4 the address contained in the PC (program counter) is copied to the MAR (memory address register) via the address bus 2 the address part of the instruction is placed in the MAR (memory address register) 6 the value in the PC (program counter) is then incremented so that it points to the next instruction to be fetched 5* The incrementation of the program counter can appear at any stage after 2. All other stages must be in the correct given order. [6]

question_text: Check digits are used to ensure the accuracy of entered data. A 7-digit number has an extra digit on the right, called the check digit. digit position: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 digit: â€“ â€“ â€“ â€“ â€“ â€“ â€“ â€“ check digit The check digit is calculated as follows: â€¢ each digit in the number is multiplied by its digit position â€¢ the seven results are then added together â€¢ this total is divided by 11 â€¢ the remainder gives the check digit (if the remainder = 10, the check digit is X) (a) Calculate the check digit for the following number. Show all your working. 4 2 4 1 5 0 8 â€¦ Check digit [2] (b) An operator has just keyed in the following number: 3 2 4 0 0 4

subject: Computer Science
question_text: A company has a number of offices around the world. (a) Data is transmitted between the offices over the Internet. In order to keep the data safe the company is using Secure Socket Layer (SSL) protocol and a firewall at each office. Explain how SSL protocol and a firewall will keep the companyâ€™s data safe. SSL protocol Firewall [4] (b) A company stores personal details of its customers on a computer system behind a firewall. Explain, with reasons, what else the company should do to keep this data safe. [6] 10 0478/12/M/J/17
mark_scheme_text:
8(a) 2 marks for SSL, 2 marks for Firewall SSL protocol Two from: Â• uses encryption Â• encryption is asymmetric / symmetric / both Â• makes use of (public and private) keys Â• data is meaningless (without decryption key / if intercepted) Firewall Two from: Â• helps prevent unauthorised access // helps prevent hacking Â• checks that data meets criteria // identifies when data does not meet criteria Â• acts as a filter for (incoming and outgoing) data // blocks any unacceptable data //allows acceptable data through 4 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 7 of 9 Question Answer Marks

8(b) Six from: Encrypt the data Â« Â« so it cannot be understood by those not entitled to view it Password protected / biometrics Â« Â« to help prevent unauthorised access Virus checking software Â« Â« helps prevent data corruption or deletion Â« identifies / removes a virus in the system Â« scans a system for viruses Spyware checking software Â« Â« helps prevent data being stolen/copied/logged Â« scans a system for spyware Drop-down input methods / selectable features Â« Â« to reduce risk of spyware / keylogging Physical method e.g. locked doors / CCTV timeout / auto log off Â« to help prevent unauthorised access Network / company policies // training employees Â« to educate users how to be vigilant Access rights Â« Â« allows users access to data that they have permission to view Â« prevents users from accessing data that they do not have permission to view 6 Question Answer Marks 9 Six from: Â• temperature sensor Â• analogue data / temperature is converted to digital data (with an ADC) Â• sensor sends signal to the microprocessor Â• microprocessor compares input values with stored values/pre-set values Â« Â• Â« if the temperature value input is too high/low Â« Â• Â« a signal is sent from the microprocessor to turn on / off / up / down the cooling unit Â• Â« if temperature matches the stored values Â« Â• Â« no action is taken Â• an actuator is used to turn the cooling unit on / off / up / down Â• the process is a continuous loop 6 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 8 of 9 Question Answer Marks

question_text: Parity checks are often used to detect errors that may occur during data transmission. The received bytes in the table below were transmitted using odd parity . Tick ( 3 ) to show whether each byte has been corrupted during transmission or not corrupted during transmission . Received byte corrupted during transmission ( 3 ) not corrupted during transmission ( 3 ) 10110100 01101101 10000001 [3] (b) Another method of error detection is Automatic Repeat reQuest (ARQ). Explain how ARQ is used in error detection. [4] 6 0478/13/M/J/17
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) 1 mark per correct tick Received byte corrupted during transmission ( 9 ) not corrupted during transmission ( 9 ) 10110100 9 01101101 9 10000001 9 3

5(b) Four from: Â• Uses acknowledgement and time out Â• Check performed on received data // error is detected by e.g. parity check, check sum Â• If error detected, request sent to resend data // negative acknowledgment is used Â• If no acknowledgement is sent that data is received // positive acknowledgement is used Â• Data is resent / Resend request repeated, till data is resent correctly Â« Â• Â« or request times out // limit is reached 4 Question Answer Marks 6 1 mark for correct bus name and up to 2 further marks for appropriate purpose. Address (bus) Two from: Â• Carries / transports an address / location Â« Â• Â« of the next item to be fetched Â• Data travels one way (unidirectional) Data (bus) Two from: Â• Carries / transports data / example of data ... Â• Â« that is currently being processed // that will be / has been processed Â• Data can travel in both directions (bidirectional) Control (bus) Two from: Â• Carries / transports signals Â• Control / directs the actions of the CPU / processor Â• Can be either Unidirectional or Bidirectional 6 0478/13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 5 of 8 Question Answer Marks 7 1 mark for correct line till 5 marks given. 5 Question Answer Marks 8 Â• Secondary Â• HDD/SSD Â• SSD/HDD Â• Primary Â• ROM/RAM Â• RAM/ROM 6 0478/13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 6 of 8 Question Answer Marks 9 1 mark for appropriate device name and 1 further mark for appropriate purpose. Input devices Two from: Â• Keypad / Keyboard Â« Â• Â« e.g. to allow customer to input the quantity of an item Â• Touchscreen Â« Â• Â« e.g. to allow a customer to select a payment method Â• Barcode scanner / Barcode reader Â« Â• Â« e.g. to allow a customer to scan in their shopping Â• Card reader // Cash deposit / intake Â« Â• Â« e.g. to allow a customer to pay for their shopping Â• Weighing scales Â« Â• Â« e.g. to allow a customer to weigh fresh produce Output devices One from: Â• Display / Touchscreen Â« Â• Â« e.g. to allow a customer to see the running total of their shopping Â• Speaker Â« Â• Â« e.g. to give audio instructions to a customer about how to use the self-checkout Â• Printer Â« Â• Â« e.g. to print a receipt for the customer 6 Question Answer Marks

Two from: Â• Gas (sensor) Â« Â• Â« e.g. to measure the levels of oxygen/carbon dioxide / nitrogen in the factory to make sure they are not too high / low Â• Temperature (sensor) Â« Â• Â« e.g. to measure the temperature of the chemicals to make sure it is not too high/low Â• Motion / Infra-red (sensor) Â« Â• Â« e.g. to detect any persons in an unauthorised area of the factory Â• Pressure (sensor) Â« Â• Â« e.g. to measure the pressure of chemicals flowing through pipes to check that level are not too high / low Â• pH (sensor) Â« Â• Â« to measure the pH to make sure the acidity / alkalinity of the chemicals is correct Â• Light (sensor) Â« Â• Â« to measure the level of light to make sure it remains at a constant level for the chemical process 4

The entry cost to the park is $30 for each student. The theme park gives one free ticket for every ten that are bought, which must be taken into consideration. Set up a program that: â€¢ stores the cost of the coach â€¢ stores the cost of an entry ticket â€¢ inputs the estimated number of students taking part, this must be validated on entry and an unsuitable entry rejected â€¢ calculates and outputs the recommended cost per student to ensure the trip does not make a loss. TASK 2 â€“ Record the students who are going and whether they have paid. Input and store the names of the students who have asked to go on the trip up to the maximum number allowed. Pre-release material A teacher is planning a school trip to a theme park at the end of term. You have been asked to write a program to work out the cost per student, to record those who are going and whether they have paid. The maximum number of students who can go on the trip is 45. Write and test a program for the teacher. â€¢ Your program must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. TASK 1 â€“ Work out the cost. The cost of the trip for each student is a share of the cost of a coach plus the cost of entry to the theme park. The total cost of the coach will be $550. The entry cost to the park is $30 for each student. The theme park gives one free ticket for every ten that are bought, which must be taken into consideration. Set up a program that: â€¢ stores the cost of the coach â€¢ stores the cost of an entry ticket â€¢ inputs the estimated number of students taking part, this must be validated on entry and an unsuitable entry rejected â€¢ calculates and outputs the recommended cost per student to ensure the trip does not make a loss. TASK 2 â€“ Record the students who are going and whether they have paid. Input and store the names of the students who have asked to go on the trip up to the maximum number allowed. Input and store whether each student has paid. Enable printouts to be produced to show students who have not paid and those who have paid. TASK 3 â€“ Work out final costs. Not al

subject: Computer Science
question_text: Optical storage media can be used to store data. Describe how the data is read from a Compact Disc (CD). [4] (b) Kamil wants to store a 16-bit colour image file. The image size is 1000 pixels. Calculate the size of the file. Give your answer in bytes . Show your working. Working Answer bytes [2] (c) Describe the differences between primary and secondary storage. [4] 11 0478/11/O/N/17
mark_scheme_text:
9(a) Any four from: â€“ (Red) laser is used â€“ (Laser beams) shines onto surface of the disk â€“ It is rotated (at a constant speed) to be read â€“ Surface is covered in a track (that spirals from the centre) â€“ Data is represented on the surface using pits and lands â€“ Pits and lands represent binary values â€“ Pits reflect light back differently (to the area in between/land) â€“ Optical device can determine the binary value from the light reflection 4

9(b) 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for correct answer: â€“ 1000 â‹… 16 â€“ 16000/8 â€“ Answer is 2000 bytes 2

9(c) Four from: (Max 2 for either primary or secondary) â€“ Primary RAM and ROM â€“ Secondary HDD and SSD â€“ Primary is directly accessible by CPU â€“ Secondary is not directly accessible by CPU â€“ Primary is internal to computer â€“ Secondary can be internal or external to the computer â€“ Primary stores boot up instructions and can hold data whilst being processed â€“ Secondary stores files/software â€“ Primary has faster access speed â€“ Secondary has a slower access speed â€“ Primary has both volatile and non-volatile â€“ Secondary is non-volatile 4 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 10 of 10 Question Answer Marks 10 1 mark for each correct tick Statement true ( 9 ) false ( 9 ) Assembly language uses mnemonic codes. 9 Assembly language programs do not need a translator to be executed. 9 Assembly language is a low-level programming language. 9 Assembly language is specific to the computer hardware. 9 Assembly language is machine code. 9 Assembly language is often used to create drivers for hardware. 9 6

[4] (b) Explain the process of converting the binary number 1010 into a denary number. [5] 4 0478/12/O/N/17
mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Any four from ( Max 2 per number system) : Â• A binary number system is a base-2 system Â• A denary number system is a base-10 system Â• A binary number system uses 0 and 1 values Â• A denary number system uses 0 to 9 values Â• A binary number system has units/ placeholders/column headings that increase by the power of 2 Â• A denary number system has units/ placeholders/column headings that increase by the power of 10 Â• Binary has more digit for the same value// Denary has less digits for the same value 4 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 3 of 9 Question Answer Marks

3(b) Five from: Â• Correct column headings / place holders by example Â• Correctly place a 1 or a 0 for each column Â• Identify the columns to be added Â• Add together the (denary) values identified Â« Â• Â« this will give a total which is the denary number/answer Â• Answer is 10 5 Question Answer Marks

subject: Computer Science
question_text: A file server is used as a central data store for a network of computers. Rory sends data from his computer to a file server that is approximately 100 metres away. It is important that the data is transmitted accurately. Rory needs to be able to read data from and write data to the file server at the same time. (a) (i) Use ticks ( 3 ) to identify the most suitable data transmission methods for this application. Method
mark_scheme_text:
4(a)(i) Method 1 Tick ( 9 ) Method 2 Tick ( 9 ) Serial 9 Simplex Parallel Half-duplex Duplex 9 2

4(a)(ii) Any four from ( Max 3 for serial): Â• Serial has less/lower interference Â• Serial is (more) reliable/accurate over distances Â• In serial the bits wonâ€™t be skewed Â• In serial it is easier to collate the bits together again after transmission Â• Duplex transmits data in both directions at the same time Â• simplex/half-duplex/remaining methods wonâ€™t allow read and write at same time 4 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 4 of 9 Question Answer Marks

4(b) 1 mark for error checking method, 2 marks for description: Checksum Â• A value is calculated from the data // Description of calculation Â• Value is transmitted with data Â• Value is recalculated after transmission Â• If the values match the data is (more likely to be) accurate Parity check Â• A parity bit is transmitted with each byte of data Â• Odd or even (parity can be used) Â• Counts / checks number of 1â€™s // counts / checks to see if 1â€™s are even // counts / checks to see if 1â€™s are odd Â• (Each byte is) checked after transmission to see if it matches the odd/even parity used Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) Â• Uses acknowledgement and timeout Â• When a device detects an error in data transmission it asks for the packet to be resent / no error detected, positive acknowledgment sent Â• The sending device resends the packet after the request to resend/ timeout received Â• This process is continuous until the packet received is correct/until the ARQ limit is reached Echo (check) Â• Copy of data is sent back to sender Â• Data is compared to see if it matches Â• If it does not match error detected 6 0478/12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 5 of 9 Question Answer Marks

4(b) 1 mark for correct compression, 3 marks for description: âˆ’ Lossless (compression) Any three from: âˆ’ The file can be restored/decompressed to the exact same state it was before compression/ to original âˆ’ (It is a computer program so) no data can be lost // Lossy would remove data âˆ’ Will not run correctly (with any other compression) âˆ’ (Lossless) will give repeating words/sections of word a value// RLE is used // Other valid examples of methods of lossless compression âˆ’ Value is recorded in an index 4 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 5 1 mark for each correct line, up to a maximum of 5 marks: Component Description Immediate access store (IAS) Holds data and instructions when they are loaded from main memory and are waiting to be processed. question_text: A supermarket uses a barcode scanner to read the barcodes on its products. (a) Describe how the barcode scanner reads the barcode. Explain how the infrared touch screen detects a userâ€™s touch. [4] 9 0478/11/M/J/18 [Turn over (d) The supermarket uses secondary storage and off-line storage to store data about its stock. Explain what is meant by secondary storage and off-line storage. Secondary storage Off-line storage [4]
mark_scheme_text:
8(a) Any four from: âˆ’ Shines light / (red) laser at barcode âˆ’ Light is called an illuminator âˆ’ Light is reflected back // White lines reflect light // Black lines reflect less light/absorbs light âˆ’ Sensors / photoelectric cells detect the light âˆ’ Different reflections / bars will give different binary values / digital values // pattern converted to digital values âˆ’ A microprocessor interprets the data 4

8(b) Any three from: âˆ’ barcode identifies a (unique) product âˆ’ barcode can be used to look up product (in a database) âˆ’ data about stock levels can be stored on a system âˆ’ stock can be automatically deducted from the system âˆ’ can check stock is below a certain level // check stock level âˆ’ automatic re-order // Alerts when stock is low âˆ’ automatically update new stock level âˆ’ to locate if an item of stock is available in another location 3 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 10 of 12 Question Answer Marks

8(c) Any four from: âˆ’ (Infrared) rays are sent across screen (from the edges) âˆ’ Has sensors around edge // Sensors capture beams âˆ’ (Infrared) rays form a grid across the screen âˆ’ (Infrared) ray is broken (by a finger blocking a beam) âˆ’ Calculation is made (on where beam is broken) to locate the â€˜touchâ€™ // Co-ordinates are used to locate the touch 4

8(d) Secondary Storage â€“ any two from: âˆ’ Not directly accessed by the CPU âˆ’ Non-volatile storage âˆ’ Secondary is internal to the computer/device âˆ’ An example of secondary storage would be HDD/SSD Off-line storage â€“ any two from: âˆ’ Non-volatile storage âˆ’ Off-line storage is storage that is removable from a computer/device // not internal // portable âˆ’ An example of off-line storage would be CD/DVD/USB stick/SD card/magnetic tape/ external HDD/SSD 4 Question Answer Marks 9 Any six from: âˆ’ Suitable biometric device, such as fingerprint scanner/retina/eye/iris scanner/face recognition/voice recognition/palm scanner // description of use e.g. use fingerprint on device âˆ’ Sensor (in biometric device) captures/takes data/readings (of user) âˆ’ Data/readings are converted from analogue to digital (using ADC) âˆ’ Data/reading sent to the microprocessor âˆ’ Data/readings compared to stored values/data Â« âˆ’ Â« if data/readings match user can enter âˆ’ Â« if data/readings do not match user is declined entry // user asked to try again Â« âˆ’ Â« alert may be sent to security // alarm may sound 6 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 11 of 12 Question Answer Marks

mark_scheme_text:
10(a) Any four from: âˆ’ Structure and presentation are defined using (mark-up) tags âˆ’ Structure and presentation dictate the appearance of the website âˆ’ Structure is used for layout âˆ’ Example of structure âˆ’ Presentation is used for formatting / style âˆ’ Example of formatting âˆ’ Separate file / CSS can be used for presentation content 4

10(b)(i) 1 mark for each correct part âˆ’ domain (name) âˆ’ file name/webpage name 2

10(b)(ii) Any two from: âˆ’ Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure // it is the access protocol // It is a protocol âˆ’ It means the website uses SSL/TLS âˆ’ It means data sent (to and from the webserver) is encrypted 2

10(c) Any two from e.g. : âˆ’ To store items that a customer has added to an online shopping basket âˆ’ To store a customerâ€™s credit card details âˆ’ To store log-in details âˆ’ To track what product a customer browses // Track music preferences âˆ’ Targeted advertising // making recommendations âˆ’ Personalises/customises the experience âˆ’ Shows who are new and returning customers âˆ’ To speed up log-in times âˆ’ To speed up/allow single click purchases âˆ’ Improves the experience 2 0478/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 12 of 12 Question Answer Marks

10(d) Any four from: âˆ’ Prevents direct access to the webserver // Sits between user and webserver âˆ’ If an attack is launched it hits the proxy server instead // can be used to help prevent DDOS // help prevent hacking of webserver âˆ’ Used to direct invalid traffic away from the webserver âˆ’ Traffic is examined by the proxy server // Filters traffic âˆ’ If traffic is valid the data from the webserver will be obtained by the user âˆ’ If traffic is invalid the request to obtain data is declined âˆ’ Can block requests from certain IP addresses 4

Every cow has a unique 3-digit identity code. Each cow can be milked twice a day, seven days a week. The volume of milk from each cow is recorded in litres correct to one decimal place (yield) every time the cow is milked. The size of the herd is fixed. At the end of the week the total and the average yield for each cow for that week is calculated. The farmer identifies the cow that has produced the most milk that week. The farmer also identifies any cows that have produced less than 12 litres of milk on four or more days that week. A program is required to record the yield for each cow every time it is milked, calculate the total weekly volume of milk for the herd and the average yield per cow in a week. The program must also identify the cow with the best yield that week and identify any cows with a yield of less than 12 litres of milk for four or more days that week. Write and test a program or programs for the farmer. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. TASK 1 â€“ Record the yield. Write a program for TASK 1 to record the milk yields for a week. The program records and stores the identity code number and the yield every time a cow is milked

Cars cannot stay overnight; the system is reset at midnight. Write and test a program or programs for the car park manager. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. TASK 1 â€“ Operating the car park. The system is reset at midnight every day. Set up a system using arrays and with suitable prompts that will carry out the following as cars enter or leave the car park: On Entry: â€¢ display the number of empty car park spaces â€¢ issue the next available ticket number â€¢ store the current time and the ticket number â€¢ display the updated number of empty car park spaces. On Exit: â€¢ input a ticket number and departure time â€¢ output the amount of time the car stayed at the car par

Use the pre-release material and your experience from attempting the tasks before the examination to answer Question 1. Pre-release material An auction company has an interactive auction board at their sale rooms, which allows buyers to place bids at any time during the auction. Before the auction starts, the sellers place their items in the sale room with a unique number attached to each item (item number). The following details about each item need to be set up on the interactive auction board system: item number, number of bids, description and reserve price. The number of bids is initially set to zero. During the auction, buyers can look at the items in the sale room and then place a bid on the interactive auction board at the sale room. Each buyer is given a unique number for identification (buyer number). All the buyer needs to do is enter their buyer number, the item number and their bid. Their bid must be greater than any existing bids. At the end of the auction, the company checks all the items and marks those that have bids greater than the reserve as sold. Any items sold will incur a fee of 10% of the final bid to be paid to the auction company. Write and test a program or programs for the auction company. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data, data must be validated on entry. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. question_text: Problem-solving and Programming May/June 2021 1 hour 45 minutes You must answer on the question paper. No additional materials are needed. INSTRUCTIONS â— Answer all questions. â— Do not attempt Tasks 1, 2 and 3 in the copy of the pre-release material on page 2; these are for information only. â— Use a black or dark blue pen. You may use an HB pencil for any diagrams or graphs. â— Write your name, centre number and candidate number in the boxes at the top of the page. â— Write your answer to each question in the space provided. â— Do not use an erasable pen or correction fluid. â— Do not write on any bar codes. â— Calculators must not be used in this paper. INFORMATION â— The total mark for this paper is 50. â— The number of marks for each question or part question is shown in brackets [ ]. â— No marks will be awarded for using brand names of software packages or hardware. * 0 3 3 7 1 8 6 9 5 9 * DC (LK) 202758/1 [Turn over 2 0478/22/M/J/21 Section A You are advised to spend no longer than 40 minutes answering this section. Here is a copy of the pre-release material. DO NOT attempt Tasks 1, 2 and 3 now. Use the pre-release material and your experience from attempting the tasks before the examination to answer Question 1. Pre-release material An electric mountain railway makes four return trips every day. In each trip the train goes up the mountain and back down. The train leaves from the foot of the mountain at 09:00, 11:00, 13:00 and 15:00. The train returns from the top of the mountain at 10:00, 12:00, 14:00 and 16:00. Each train has six coaches with eighty seats available in each coach. Passengers can only purchase a return ticket; all tickets must be purchased on the day of travel. The cost is $25 for the journey up and $25 for the journey down. Groups of between ten and eighty passengers inclusive get a free ticket for every tenth passenger, provided they all travel together (every tenth passenger travels free). Passengers must book their return train journey, as well as the departure train journey, when they purchase their ticket. Passengers can return on the next train down the mountain or a later train. The last train from the top of the mountain has two extra coaches on it. The train times are displayed on a large screen, together with the number of tickets still available for each train. Every time a ticket is booked the display is updated. When a train is full, the word â€˜Closedâ€™ is displayed instead of the number of tickets available. Write and test a program or programs for the electric mountain railway. â€¢ Your program or programs must include appropriate prompts for the entry of data; data must be validated on entry. â€¢ Error messages and other output need to be set out clearly and understandably. â€¢ All variables, constants and other identifiers must have meaningful names. You will need to complete these three tasks. Each task must be fully tested. Task 1 â€“ Start of the day. Write a program to set up the screen display for the start of the day.

6(a)(ii) Four from ( MAX 2 per translator): ï‚· An interpreter translates the code line by line (and executes each line immediately) ï‚· â€¦ whereas a compiler translates the whole code at the same time (before executing it) ï‚· A compiler produces an executable file ï‚· â€¦ but an interpreter does not ï‚· An interpreter is required to run the code each time if used ï‚· â€¦ whereas a compiler is not ï‚· An interpreter stops and reports an error as it is encountered ï‚· â€¦ whereas a compiler creates a report of all errors at the end of translation ï‚· An interpreter will run code up to the point it finds an error ï‚· â€¦ whereas a compiler will not run the code at all if an error is found 4 Question Answer Marks

question_text: A computer can have both a Media Access Control (MAC) address and an Internet Protocol (IP) address. (a) Give two similarities between a MAC address and an IP address. Similarity
mark_scheme_text:
8(a) Any two from: â€¢ They are both unique addresses â€¢ They can both be used to identify a device (on a network) â€¢ They are both assigned to hardware â€¢ They can both be represented as hexadecimal 2

8(b) Any two from: e.g. â€¢ A MAC address is assigned by the manufacturer, whereas an IP address is assigned by the network/router/ISP â€¢ A MAC address is represented as hexadecimal, whereas an IP address can sometimes be represented as numeric â€¢ A MAC address is normally static, whereas an IP address can be dynamic â€¢ A MAC address has 6 groups of digits, whereas an IP address has 4/8 groups â€¢ A MAC address is 6 bytes (48 bit), whereas an IP address is 4/ 16 bytes (32/128 bit) 2 0478 / 13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 14 of 15 Question Answer Marks 9 Any six from (MAX four for ARQ): â€¢ Odd or even parity is set/agreed for the data â€¢ A parity bit is added to each byte of data â€¢ â€¦ to make the number of 1s match parity â€¢ Data is checked after transmission to see if parity is correct â€¢ ARQ uses acknowledgement and timeout â€¢ If no error is found, a positive acknowledgement is sent to the sender / no acknowledgement is sent to the sender â€¢ If an error is found, a negative acknowledgement is sent to the sender â€¦ â€¢ â€¦ that triggers the data to be resent â€¢ When the data is sent, a timer is started â€¢ If an acknowledgement is not received within the time set, the data is resent .. . â€¢ â€¦ until an acknowledgement is received / resend limit is reached 6 0478 / 13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 20 22 22 Page 15 of 15 Question Answer Marks

subject: Computer Science
question_text: A farmer uses an automated robot to plant seeds in the field. (a) State what is meant by the robot being automated. [1] (b) Give three characteristics of a robot. 1 2 3 [3] (c) The robot plants seeds and stops when it reaches a fence. It then turns and continues planting seeds. The robot uses sensors and a microprocessor to know when it reaches a fence. Explain how the robot uses sensors and a microprocessor to know it has reached a fence. [6] 9 0478/11/M/J/24 [Turn over (d) Give two advantages of the farmer using an automated robot to plant seeds. 1 2 [2] (e) Give two disadvantages of the farmer using an automated robot to plant seeds. 1 2 [2] (f) The robot is adapted to have machine learning capabilities. Explain how this will improve the robot. [2] 10 0478/11/M/J/24

8(a) The robot can perform actions without human intervention 1

question_text: A barcode scanning system uses a check digit to check for errors in data on input. (a) Explain how the barcode scanning system operates to check for errors. [4] (b) After a barcode is scanned, data is sent to a stock control system to update the stock value stored for that product. The data is sent to the stock control system using serial simplex data transmission. (i) Explain how the data is sent using serial simplex data transmission. [3] * 0000800000006 * , , Ä¬Ã™ÃºÂ¾Ä Â´Ã­ÃˆÃµÃÄªÃ…ÄŠÃŸÃºÎ¼Ã¾Ã— Ä¬Ã§Â°Ã³ÃÄÄ¤Ä¬ÃŽÄˆÃ¶Ã„Ä¡Â²Ã¼Ã±Ä—Ä‚ Ä¥Ä…Ä•Ä•ÃµÎ¼Ã…Ä•Î¼ÃµÃµÄ…Ä…Î¼Ã¥Ã•ÃµÃ• DO NOT WRITE IN THIS MARGIN DO NOT WRITE IN THIS MARGIN DO NOT WRITE IN THIS MARGIN DO NOT WRITE IN THIS MARGIN DO NOT WRITE IN THIS MARGIN 7 0478/12/O/N/24 [Turn over (ii) Explain why serial simplex is the most appropriate method of data transmission for this purpose. [3] (iii) The data is checked for errors after it has been transmitted to the stock control system. Give two error detection methods that could be used for this purpose. 1 2 [2]
mark_scheme_text:
5(a) Any four from: â€¢ A check digit is calculated from/using the barcode data â€¢ â€¦ using an algorithm // by example e.g. Modulo 11 â€¢ â€¦ and added to the barcode â€¢ When/after the barcode is scanned the check digit is recalculated â€¦ â€¢ â€¦ using the same algorithm â€¢ If the check digits do not match an error has occurred when scanning the barcode // If the check digits match no error has occurred when scanning the barcode 4

5(b)(i) â€¢ Bits are sent one at a time â€¢ Bits are sent down a single wire â€¢ Data is sent in one direction only 3 0478 / 12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 8 of 11 Question Answer Marks

5(b)(ii) Any three from: â€¢ The stock control system may be a long distance away â€¢ â€¦ parallel should not be used in long distance transmission // Serial is more reliable for long distance transmission â€¢ The data does not need to be sent quickly â€¢ â€¦ the increased speed of parallel is not needed â€¢ â€¦ as only small amounts of data need to be sent â€¢ The bits are sent/arrived in order â€¢ â€¦ the data will not be skewed // the data could be skewed if parallel was used â€¢ â€¦ there will be no data collisions â€¢ There will be less interference/crosstalk (due to single wire) â€¢ â€¦ there will be fewer errors in the data â€¢ No need for a reply/response from stock control system â€¢ â€¦ half - duplex/full - duplex is not necessary as only one way transmission needed 3

5(b)(iii) Any two from: â€¢ (Odd/even) Parity check // Parity byte check // parity block check â€¢ Checksum â€¢ Echo check â€¢ (Positive/negative) ARQ // Automatic repeat query // Automatic repeat request 2 0478 / 12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 9 of 11 Question Answer Marks 6 One mark for each correct term or definition in the correct place: Components â€¢ Control unit // CU â€¢ Memory address register // MAR â€¢ Data bus â€¢ Current instruction register // CIR Descriptions â€¢ (Program counter) Stores the address of the next instruction to be fetched â€¢ (Accumulator) Stores the interim result for a calculation 6 âˆ’ Question Answer Marks

11(d) One mark per mark point â€¢ Data from 4 correct columns printed â€“ any number of rows, any order â€¢ Data in rows ordered as shown (row and column) at least two rows and two columns required â€¢ All data correct with no extra content Correct output 104S Single 1 72.50 105S Single 1 72.50 201F Family 4 160.00 301D Double 2 200.00 304P Suite 6 700.00 3 0478 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 11 of 14 Question Answer Marks 12 â€¢ AO2 (maximum 9 marks) â€¢ AO3 (maximum 6 marks) Data Structures required with names as given in the scenario: Arrays or lists Rooms[] , Dimensions[] Variables Number Requirements (techniques): R1 Input and store number of rooms, the names of the rooms and their dimensions, including validation of number of rooms (input with prompts, (nested) iteration, use of variables, 1D and 2D arrays, validation). R2 Calculate and store the area of each room, the total area of the house and the average room area rounded to two decimal places. Find the smallest and largest rooms (calculation, totalling, rounding, finding maximum and minimum values, iteration). Example 15 - mark answer in pseudocode // input and validation of number of rooms REPEAT OUTPUT "How many rooms are in your house (enter a number between 3 and 20 inclusive)?" INPUT Number IF Number < 3 OR Number > 20 THEN OUTPUT "The number of rooms must be between 3 and 20 inclusive, please try again" ENDIF UNTIL Number >= 3 AND Number <= 20 // input of room names and dimensions â€“ could be more than one loop FOR InLoop ï‚¬ 1 TO Number OUTPUT "Enter the name of room ", InLoop INPUT Rooms[InLoop] OUTPUT "Enter the length of the room in metres" INPUT Dimensions[InLoop, 1] OUTPUT "Enter the width of the room in metres" INPUT Dimensions[InLoop, 2] Dimensions[InLoop, 3] ï‚¬ ROUND(Dimensions[InLoop, 1] * Dimensions[InLoop, 2], 2) NEXT InLoop // calculates total area and average area â€“ rounded values have been stored TotArea ï‚¬ 0 FOR Total ï‚¬ 1 TO Number TotArea ï‚¬ TotArea + Dimensions[Total, 3] NEXT Total AvArea ï‚¬ ROUND(TotArea / Number, 2) 15 0478 / 22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2024 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2024 Page 12 of 14 Question Answer Marks 12 // finding largest and smallest rooms Larea ï‚¬ 0 Sarea ï‚¬ 100000 Lindex ï‚¬ 1 Sindex ï‚¬ 1 FOR Count ï‚¬ 1 TO Number IF Dimensions[Count, 3] > Larea THEN Larea ï‚¬ Dimensions[Count, 3] Lindex ï‚¬ Count ENDIF IF Dimensions[Count, 3] < Sarea THEN Sarea ï‚¬ Dimensions[Count, 3] Sindex ï‚¬ Count ENDIF NEXT C

3(b)(i) One mark for each correct term in the correct place. â€¢ w hole code â€¢ e xecut ing â€¢ a ll â€¢ l ine by line â€¢ e rror A compiler translates the whole code at once before executing it. A compiler produces an error report that displays all errors. An interpreter translates and executes the code line by line . 3(b)(ii) One mark for each correct function. One mark for each correct matching role description. Examples: â€¢ Cod e editor â€¢ Allows the programmer to write /change the program. â€¢ Run - time environment â€¢ Allows the user to run the code and see the output. â€¢ Error diagnostics â€¢ Features that can be used to find errors in the code â€¢ Auto - completion â€¢ A programmer starts to type a command word, and the IDE suggests option for completing it. â€¢ Auto - correction â€¢ If a programmer misspells a command word it is changed to the correct spelling â€¢ Prettyprint â€¢ The command words / identifiers are given different colours 4

3(c)(i) Any one from: â€¢ Inkjet â€¢ Laser 1 0478 / 12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 10 of 15 Question Answer Marks

3(c)(ii) One mark from: â€¢ Serial â€¢ Parallel One mark from: â€¢ Half - duplex â€¢ Full - duplex â€¢ Simplex Any four from (for descriptions matching transmission types given): serial â€¢ Serial would send bits in order // serial uses only one wire. â€¢ â€¦ so wonâ€™t be skewed // less likely to have errors â€¢ Serial transmission speed would be adequate. parallel â€¢ Parallel would transmit data faster. â€¢ â€¦ as multiple bits are sent at the same time // â€¦ as multiple wires are used. â€¢ For parallel , c hance of skewing/errors would be low as short distance transmission only required. h alf/full duplex â€¢ To allow data to be sent in both directions â€¢ â€¦ so a ny interrupts/notifications for errors can be sent back to the computer. s implex â€¢ Data only needs to be sent one direction // Data transmission doesnâ€™t need to be two - way. â€¢ â€¦ as the printer may not need to send errors back to the computer 6 0478 / 12 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 11 of 15 Question Answer Marks

3(c)(iii) â€¢ A parity bit is added to each byte. â€¢ â€¦ to make the number of 1s/0s even // that will be 1 if the number of 1s/0s is odd // that will be 0 if the number of 1s/0s is even. â€¢ The number of 1s /0s in each byte is counted after transmission. â€¢ I f any bytes have an odd number of 1s/0s an error is detected 4 Question Answer Mark s

subject: Computer Science
question_text: A collector needs a video library system for a collection of videos stored on 4K Blu-ray discs, standard Blu-ray discs, DVD, or as digital downloads. The two-dimensional (2D) array Video[] is used to store, for each video item, the title, the format of the video (4K Blu-ray disc, standard Blu-ray disc, DVD, or digital download), the year it was released stored as a string and a storage code to represent where the video can be found in the collection, for example: Video[1,1] is "Macbeth" Video[1,2] is "digital download" Video[1,3] is "2015" Video[1,4] is "DG276" The two-dimensional (2D) array Results[] is used to store the results of a search to find a specific video. The search uses the video title, and if the video is found, its data is copied from the array Video[] to the array Results[]. The search continues and if other videos with the same title are found, this data is also copied to the array Results[]. The search ends when the end of the data is reached. Write a program that meets the following requirements: â€¢ The video library array needs to be initialised with the null string (""), allowing for up to 10 000 records. The array Results[] must be initialised before every search with the null string and must be able to hold up to 20 search results with the same title. â€¢ Create a menu to add a new video to the library to search for an existing video by title, or to stop, with validation of the input. â€¢ When the user enters a new video, the following data is stored in the first available location of the relevant array: o video title o format (4K Blu-ray disc, standard Blu-ray disc, DVD, digital download) o year of release as string o storage code. â€¢ Allow input of data for another video if required. â€¢ When searching for an existing video, if a match is found, transfer all the data for that video into the array Results[], and continue to search until all videos with the same title have been found. â€¢ Output the results from the array Results[] â€¢ If the video title is not found, output a suitable message. â€¢ The system returns to the menu after completing the input or the output, until the user chooses to stop. You must use pseudocode or program code and add comments to explain how your code works. You do not need to declare any arrays, variables or constants. You may assume that this has already been done.
